normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.source_section: MASVS-CODE: Code Quality and Build Settings — MASVS-CODE-3 (page 28)
normative_source.requirement_title: The app only uses software components without known vulnerabilities.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-CODE-3 (Description): To be truly secure, a full whitebox assessment should have been performed on all app components. However, as it usually happens with e.g. for third-party components this is not always feasible and not typically part of a penetration test. This control covers "low-hanging fruit" cases, such as those that can be detected just by scanning libraries for known vulnerabilities.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must avoid shipping software components (third-party libraries, SDKs, native dependencies) with publicly-disclosed vulnerabilities. The control covers the detectable-by-scanning subset: components with CVEs in the National Vulnerability Database (NVD), OSV (Open Source Vulnerabilities), GitHub Security Advisories, or vendor-published advisories. Detection happens via dependency-scanning tools (Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check, Trivy) at build time and ongoing. The control does NOT require full whitebox audit of all components — it explicitly carves out the broader audit as out of scope for routine compliance.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies at Tier 1+ as a conservative corpus-level gate. Per Constitution Pillar 3 (Cryptography & Key Material), dependency hygiene activates at Tier 1+; SEC-PILLAR-3-DEPENDENCY-PINNING is a Tier 1-5 control while SEC-PILLAR-3-SDK-PROVENANCE is Tier 3-5 (vendor security history review). The blueprint schema declares sdk_provenance (per Constitution v0.5); the broader dependency_pinning field referenced in the Constitution definition is not yet a dedicated schema field, so current evidence flows through package manifests (Podfile, Package.swift, build.gradle), committed lockfiles (Package.resolved, Podfile.lock, gradle.lockfile), reachability_notes, and dependency-scan tool outputs. A future schema enhancement (banked) will add dedicated dependency_pinning and dependency_vulnerability_scan_policy fields. The tier-1 applicability floor is honest because every modern mobile app ships third-party dependencies that may carry disclosed vulnerabilities; provenance review at Tier 3+ adds the vendor-history dimension. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 1+ requires dependency vulnerability scanning because Node modules, Pods, Gradle dependencies, and SwiftPM packages all carry potential CVE exposure.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 3 covers MASVS-CODE-3 via two supporting traces with no single dedicated primary — SEC-PILLAR-3-DEPENDENCY-PINNING (supporting, dependency version pinning + reachability analysis) and SEC-PILLAR-3-SDK-PROVENANCE (supporting, provider security history and maintenance-status review). Neither trace alone is THE control for "libraries without known vulnerabilities" by name and substance — DEPENDENCY-PINNING is about version-locking and reachability, SDK-PROVENANCE is about vendor evaluation — but together they substantively cover the MASVS-CODE-3 surface: pinned versions enable precise CVE tracking against the actually-shipped versions, and provenance review catches the case where a vendor's CVE history makes ongoing CVE risk likely. Both labeled supporting in mapping file v1.2 because Constitution distributes the coverage across two Pillar 3 controls rather than concentrating in one dedicated trace. The mapping is partial because no single Constitution trace is dedicated 1:1; the two-trace distributed coverage substantively addresses the MASVS-CODE-3 scope. Implementation correctness for the chosen mechanisms (dependency-scanning tool integration, reachability-analysis configuration to suppress unreachable-vuln noise, vendor security-history evaluation cadence, response-policy for newly-disclosed CVEs in shipping components) requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration.

expected_evidence_patterns: Dependency-scanning tool integrated in CI pipeline — Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check, Trivy, or equivalent; scan blocks merge on disclosed CVEs above declared severity threshold

iOS: Podfile and Package.swift declare versions either as exact strings or as ranges paired with a committed Package.resolved / Podfile.lock that captures the resolved version graph; vulnerability scanning evaluates the resolved shipped versions, not the declared range

Android: build.gradle declares versions either as exact strings or as ranges paired with a committed gradle.lockfile capturing the resolved version graph; vulnerability scanning evaluates the resolved shipped versions
Reachability analysis configured to distinguish unreachable-but-flagged vs reachable-and-exploitable CVE matches — suppresses noise from vulnerabilities in code paths the app does not execute

Pinning strategy declared via package-manager configuration: exact-version pinning OR range-with-committed-lockfile (Package.resolved on iOS Swift PM, Podfile.lock on iOS CocoaPods, gradle.lockfile on Android Gradle); tooling (Renovate, Dependabot) configuration drives auto-update of pinned versions; future schema field security_manifest.dependency_pinning (banked) will formalize the policy declaration

security_manifest.sdk_provenance declares per-SDK evaluation: vendor security history, maintenance status (last commit, last release, response time to disclosed CVEs), ownership, reputation; re-evaluation triggered on provider acquisition, ownership change, or unaddressed CVE
Build pipeline rejects builds with disclosed CVEs above declared severity threshold and no documented exception; exception requires reachability-analysis evidence or compensating-control declaration
failure_indicators: No dependency-scanning in build pipeline — CVE-affected versions ship without detection until external disclosure pressure

Unpinned dependencies WITHOUT committed lockfile — Gradle `+` notation without gradle.lockfile, Pod `~>` ranges without Podfile.lock committed, SwiftPM `branch:` references without Package.resolved committed — could resolve to vulnerable versions on subsequent builds without code changes and without scan-on-resolved-version coverage
Pinned dependencies to versions with known CVEs (NVD-listed, OSV-listed, GitHub Advisory) and no documented exception in security_manifest — pinning without CVE review defeats the control
Dependency-scanning configured but warnings ignored — CVE matches above declared severity surface without blocking merge or triggering remediation
SDK from unmaintained vendor (no commits or releases in 18+ months) without reachability analysis or compensating-control declaration — provenance signal indicates likely-unpatched-future-CVE
Vendor acquired by entity with poor security track record without provenance re-evaluation — Constitution v0.5 explicitly requires re-evaluation on provider acquisition / ownership change
Transitive-dependency CVEs ignored — direct dependencies pinned but transitive resolution allows vulnerable indirect dependencies through; needs full-dependency-tree analysis
false_positive_traps: Dependency handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Dev/test dependencies (Jest, JUnit, mockK, XCTest) are not shipped to production; CVEs in these are not MASVS-CODE-3 violations even when scanners flag them.

CVEs in code paths verified unreachable via reachability analysis (Snyk Code, GitHub CodeQL, or equivalent) are NOT violations as long as security_manifest documents the reachability assertion and re-runs analysis on dependency or code changes — per Constitution's "reachability analysis required" clause.

Build-time-only dependencies (lint tools, code generators, build plugins not shipped in the binary) are NOT subject to MASVS-CODE-3 even when scanners flag them — security_manifest can declare the build-time-only scope.

Dependencies with CVEs disclosed AFTER the last build but BEFORE the next release window are NOT violations if security_manifest declares the response-policy SLA (e.g., "critical CVEs trigger hotfix within 72 hours; high CVEs within 7 days") and the response-policy is being followed.

Vulnerabilities in OS-bundled libraries (system libraries the app does not ship) are NOT MASVS-CODE-3 violations — they fall under MASVS-CODE-1 (platform version floor) instead. Distinct control scope.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 3 dependency-pinning discipline (SEC-PILLAR-3-DEPENDENCY-PINNING) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-CODE-3 coverage on the version-tracking-and-reachability vector
uses Constitution Pillar 3 SDK-provenance discipline (SEC-PILLAR-3-SDK-PROVENANCE) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-CODE-3 coverage on the vendor-history-and-maintenance-status vector

declares dependency hygiene via package manifests, committed lockfiles, security_manifest.sdk_provenance, reachability_notes, and dependency-scan tool integration per MASVS-CODE-3 platform-best-practice requirement; future schema enhancement (banked) adds dedicated dependency_pinning and dependency_vulnerability_scan_policy fields
scans shipping dependencies against NVD / OSV / GitHub Advisory databases at build time with declared severity-threshold blocking
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-CODE-3. MASVS-CODE-3 is mapped to two Constitution v0.5 traces in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-3-DEPENDENCY-PINNING (supporting, version-tracking-and-reachability layer) and SEC-PILLAR-3-SDK-PROVENANCE (supporting, vendor-history layer). Both are labeled supporting because no single Constitution trace is dedicated 1:1 to MASVS-CODE-3 — Pillar 3 distributes the coverage across two Pillar 3 controls. The partial-mapping label is honest about this. Claims about known-vulnerability coverage are scoped — they apply only to the scanner databases consulted (NVD / OSV / GitHub Advisory or others as declared), the severity thresholds enforced, the resolved dependency graph at scan time, the reachability rules applied, and the scan-time snapshot; claims do NOT extend to undisclosed-zero-day vulnerabilities, post-scan-window disclosures, undeclared scanner-database gaps, or full-whitebox-audit completeness. MASVS-CODE-3 explicitly scopes to "low-hanging fruit" scanner-detectable vulnerabilities. Out of scope: OS-bundled library CVEs (covered by MASVS-CODE-1 platform-version-floor), build-time-only tooling CVEs (not shipped), and reachability-analysis-suppressed unreachable findings (require documented analysis re-runs on code changes). Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
